note_physics_4_20_0.js.title: Flux Density vs Flux
note_physics_4_20_0.js.text: Students confuse B (magnetic flux density in Tesla) with Φ (magnetic flux in Weber). Remember: B is field strength at a point (independent of area); Φ depends on the area. Φ = BA (or BAcosθ if at an angle).

note_physics_4_20_0.js.title: Right Force Direction
note_physics_4_20_0.js.text: Use Fleming's LEFT-hand rule for motor/force direction (F = BIL). Thumb = force direction; Index = B field; Middle finger = conventional current. Maximum force when wire is perpendicular to B; zero force when parallel.

note_physics_4_20_1.js.title: Flux Angle Convention Trap
note_physics_4_20_1.js.text: In Φ = BAcosθ, θ is the angle between B and the NORMAL to the coil face, NOT the angle between B and the coil plane itself. Maximum flux (Φ = BA) when B is perpendicular to coil face (θ = 0°); zero flux when parallel to face (θ = 90°).

note_physics_4_20_1.js.title: Flux vs Flux Linkage Units
note_physics_4_20_1.js.text: Flux Φ is measured in Weber (Wb); flux linkage NΦ is measured in Wb turns. When exam questions give data for flux, multiply by N before applying Faraday's law: ε = −N(ΔΦ/Δt) or ε = −Δ(NΦ)/Δt.

note_physics_4_20_2.js.title: B and Φ Must Not be Confused in Force Calculations
note_physics_4_20_2.js.text: When calculating force on a wire (F = BIL), use magnetic flux DENSITY B (in Tesla), not flux Φ. Common error: substituting Φ for B and getting units wrong. Force depends directly on B field strength, not on area or flux.

note_physics_4_20_2.js.title: Angle Effect on Force
note_physics_4_20_2.js.text: General form F = BIL sinθ where θ is angle between wire and field. Maximum force (F = BIL) when θ = 90°; zero force when wire is parallel to field (θ = 0°). Always identify whether the given angle is between wire and field, or between wire and normal.

note_physics_4_21_0.js.title: Stationary Magnet Produces No E.M.F
note_physics_4_21_0.js.text: Students often think a strong magnet held still inside a coil will induce an e.m.f. Incorrect! It is the CHANGE in flux (ΔΦ/Δt) that induces an e.m.f, not the flux itself. No motion = no change = zero e.m.f, regardless of field strength.

note_physics_4_21_0.js.title: Three Conditions for Induction (C-R-D)
note_physics_4_21_0.js.text: Remember C-R-D: Closed circuit required; Rate of change of flux linkage must be non-zero (ΔNΦ/Δt ≠ 0); Direction of induced current opposes the change (Lenz's Law). If any one fails, no induction occurs.

note_physics_4_21_1.js.title: Transformers Do NOT Work with DC
note_physics_4_21_1.js.text: A common exam pitfall: students assume transformers work with any voltage. False! Transformers require AC because they need a CHANGING magnetic flux through the iron core. DC produces a constant field → no change → no induced e.m.f in secondary → transformer is useless with DC.

note_physics_4_21_1.js.title: Ideal Transformer Power Equation
note_physics_4_21_1.js.text: For ideal transformers (100% efficient), power is conserved: Vp·Ip = Vs·Is. Combined with turns ratio Vs/Vp = Ns/Np, you can solve for any unknown. Always use this power conservation constraint in exam questions.

note_physics_4_21_2.js.title: Explain the Negative Sign in Faraday's Law
note_physics_4_21_2.js.text: Examiners regularly ask: 'Explain the negative sign in ε = −N(ΔΦ/Δt).' The answer: the negative sign represents Lenz's Law — the induced e.m.f acts to oppose the change in flux that caused it. Omitting this explanation costs marks.

note_physics_4_21_2.js.title: Lenz's Law Direction Steps
note_physics_4_21_2.js.text: Always follow three steps: (1) Identify if flux through coil is INCREASING or DECREASING. (2) State what pole the coil must become to OPPOSE this change. (3) Apply right-hand grip rule to find current direction. Marks awarded for each step stated explicitly.

note_physics_4_21_3.js.title: Graph Gradient = Induced E.M.F Magnitude
note_physics_4_21_3.js.text: On a flux linkage vs time graph, the gradient at any point equals −ε. Zero gradient = zero e.m.f; steepest gradient = greatest |ε|. Most common error: reading flux value instead of gradient slope on the exam chart.

note_physics_4_21_3.js.title: Remember to Include N (Number of Turns)
note_physics_4_21_3.js.text: Faraday's law is ε = −N(ΔΦ/Δt), not ε = −(ΔΦ/Δt). Forgetting to multiply by N is a frequent loss of marks. Always identify N separately and ensure it multiplies the flux change rate.

note_physics_4_21_4.js.title: Peak Voltage ≠ RMS Voltage in Power Calculations
note_physics_4_21_4.js.text: The most common exam error: using peak voltage V₀ in P = V²/R gives power 2× too large. Always use RMS values: P = Vrms²/R. For 230 V UK mains (RMS), peak is 325 V — using 325 in a power formula is wrong.

note_physics_4_21_4.js.title: RMS = V₀/√2 Only for Sinusoidal Waveforms
note_physics_4_21_4.js.text: The formula Vrms = V₀/√2 applies ONLY to sinusoidal AC. For square waves or other shapes, Vrms ≠ V₀/√2. Some exams give non-sinusoidal signals — check the graph carefully before applying the formula.

note_physics_4_21_5.js.title: Oscilloscope Reads Peak, Not RMS
note_physics_4_21_5.js.text: An oscilloscope displays PEAK voltage directly from the trace amplitude. To find power in a resistor, you must convert: Vrms = V₀/√2 first, then use P = Vrms²/R. Many students forget this conversion step.

note_physics_4_21_5.js.title: UK Mains Voltage Facts for Exam Memory
note_physics_4_21_5.js.text: UK mains: 230 V RMS, 325 V peak (≈ 230√2), 50 Hz frequency, 20 ms period. These values appear frequently in exam questions. Peak-to-peak voltage is 650 V — remember the danger of the live wire.

note_physics_4_22_2.js.title: Thermionic vs Photoelectric — Know the Difference
note_physics_4_22_2.js.text: Both release electrons from metal, but via different energy sources: Thermionic = HEAT energy liberates electrons; Photoelectric = LIGHT (photons) liberates electrons. The work function φ must be overcome in both cases. Exam questions test this distinction directly.

note_physics_4_22_2.js.title: Electron Gun Energy Equation
note_physics_4_22_2.js.text: For an electron accelerated through p.d. V: always use eV = ½mev². The electrical potential energy gained equals kinetic energy of the electron. At 5 kV: KE = 5 keV = 5 × 10³ × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.

note_physics_4_22_3.js.title: LINAC: Tubes Get Longer; Cyclotron: Frequency Fixed
note_physics_4_22_3.js.text: Exam trap: confusing LINAC and cyclotron operation. In a LINAC, drift tubes INCREASE in length as the ion speeds up to maintain synchronisation with fixed-frequency AC. In a cyclotron, the geometry is fixed — instead frequency controls on the dees adjust. Know the key difference.

note_physics_4_22_3.js.title: Cyclotron Resonance Condition
note_physics_4_22_3.js.text: Cyclotron frequency f = qB/(2πm) is INDEPENDENT of particle speed — this is why resonance is maintained. At relativistic speeds, particle mass increases, breaking resonance → synchrotron uses a VARYING magnetic field to maintain synchronisation.

note_physics_4_22_4.js.title: Derivation: Magnetic Force = Centripetal Force
note_physics_4_22_4.js.text: Students often forget how r = mv/(Bq) is derived. The key: magnetic force provides centripetal force: Bqv = mv²/r. Rearrange to get r. This derivation is frequently tested. Know it cold.

note_physics_4_22_4.js.title: Momentum Effect on Track Radius
note_physics_4_22_4.js.text: From r = mv/(Bq): larger momentum → larger radius. Use this to interpret particle tracks: a wider-curving track means higher momentum. For same B and q, doubling momentum doubles the radius — direct proportionality.

note_physics_4_22_5.js.title: Track Spiralling = Energy Loss
note_physics_4_22_5.js.text: A track that spirals INWARD (decreasing radius) means the particle is losing energy via ionisation. Do NOT assume constant radius in cloud chambers. Plot the energy loss via: as r decreases, v decreases (from r = mv/Bq), so kinetic energy decreases.

note_physics_4_22_5.js.title: Pair Production V-Shape Recognition
note_physics_4_22_5.js.text: A V-shaped track with NO incoming track indicates pair production: γ ray (invisible, no track) converts to e⁻ and e⁺. The two tracks curve in OPPOSITE directions — this opposite curvature is the unique signature confirming e⁻ (negative) and e⁺ (positive) charges.

note_physics_4_22_6.js.title: Collider vs Fixed-Target Efficiency
note_physics_4_22_6.js.text: A head-on collider (particles circulating in opposite directions) provides ALL collision kinetic energy for particle creation. A fixed-target (beam hits stationary target) wastes most energy carrying away recoil momentum. This is why LHC can reach quark-probing energies — a fixed-target would need ~100× more beam energy to match 13 TeV centre-of-mass.

note_physics_4_22_6.js.title: Always Verify All Conservation Laws
note_physics_4_22_6.js.text: After writing a particle collision equation, check: energy conserved, momentum conserved, charge conserved, baryon number conserved, lepton number conserved. Omitting any check costs exam marks. Use a table format to avoid errors.

note_physics_4_22_7.js.title: Why TWO Photons in Annihilation?
note_physics_4_22_7.js.text: This is a classic exam question: 'Why does e⁻ + e⁺ → γ + γ, not e⁻ + e⁺ → γ?' Answer: momentum conservation. Initial state at rest: total momentum = 0. A single photon has p = E/c ≠ 0, violating conservation. Two photons at 180° have opposite momenta that cancel: p₁ + p₂ = 0.

note_physics_4_22_7.js.title: Pair Production Threshold = 2mec² Exactly
note_physics_4_22_7.js.text: Minimum photon energy for pair production = 2 × 0.511 MeV = 1.022 MeV (when products created at rest). Any excess photon energy becomes kinetic energy of the pair. Common mistake: subtracting KE from the threshold — the threshold is fixed regardless of KE.

note_physics_4_22_8.js.title: Unit Conversion Direction Error = 10⁶ Factor Loss
note_physics_4_22_8.js.text: Most frequent student error: using 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J when working in MeV (should be 1 MeV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ J). This gives answers 10⁶ times wrong. Check the prefix (k, M, G, T) before converting.

note_physics_4_22_8.js.title: Key Rest Energies to Memorise
note_physics_4_22_8.js.text: Electron: mec² = 0.511 MeV; Proton: mpc² = 938.3 MeV. The ratio is 1836. Memorise these — they appear in nearly every particle physics exam. Pair production thresholds calculated from these values.

note_physics_4_22_9.js.title: Relativistic Speed = Calculate Lorentz Factor First
note_physics_4_22_9.js.text: If particle speed is given as a decimal of c (e.g. 0.9c, 0.6c), this is a signal to use relativistic formulae. Calculate γ = 1/√(1−v²/c²) first. Using classical KE = ½mv² at these speeds underestimates energy by factor of γ.

note_physics_4_22_9.js.title: Relativistic KE vs Classical
note_physics_4_22_9.js.text: Relativistic kinetic energy: KE = (γ−1)mc². At v = 0, γ = 1 and KE = 0 (correct). As v → c, γ → ∞ and KE → ∞ (no object can reach c). Classical ½mv² only equals (γ−1)mc² when v ≪ c and γ ≈ 1.

note_physics_4_23_0.js.title: Quark Charge Calculation Trap
note_physics_4_23_0.js.text: Up-type quarks (u, c, t): charge +2/3e. Down-type (d, s, b): charge −1/3e. Check proton uud: (+2/3) + (+2/3) + (−1/3) = +1 ✓. Check neutron udd: (+2/3) + (−1/3) + (−1/3) = 0 ✓. Reversed charges = wrong answer.

note_physics_4_23_0.js.title: Baryon = 3 Quarks; Meson = Quark-Antiquark
note_physics_4_23_0.js.text: Baryons (3 quarks) have baryon number +1 for matter, −1 for antimatter. Mesons (qq̄) have baryon number 0. This distinction is critical for conservation law checks in decay equations.

note_physics_4_23_2.js.title: Conservation Law Check Table Format Avoids Errors
note_physics_4_23_2.js.text: Write a table with rows for Q (charge), B (baryon number), L (lepton number), S (strangeness). List initial state and final state separately. Common error: forgetting to check lepton number — always include it.

note_physics_4_23_2.js.title: Strangeness Conserved in Strong/EM, Not Weak
note_physics_4_23_2.js.text: Strangeness (S) conservation depends on interaction type: strong force and electromagnetic force CONSERVE S (change = 0); weak force VIOLATES S (can change by ±1). Identify interaction type first — if neutrino involved, it's weak and S can change.

note_physics_4_23_3.js.title: One Failed Conservation Law = Reaction Forbidden
note_physics_4_23_3.js.text: A reaction is ONLY allowed if ALL quantum numbers are conserved (except strangeness in weak interactions). If even one conservation law fails, the reaction cannot occur. Students sometimes think 'close enough' — wrong! Conservation is absolute.

note_physics_4_23_3.js.title: Recognise Weak Interactions by Neutrino Emission
note_physics_4_23_3.js.text: If a decay equation includes a neutrino (νe, νμ, ντ), it is weak interaction — strangeness can change by ±1. Example: β⁻ decay n → p + e⁻ + ν̄e violates no conservation law (check: Q, B, L all conserved).
